summary: Fortieth U.S. president (1981-1989), a former Hollywood actor and Screen Actors Guild president who served two terms as Governor of California (1967-1975) before winning the presidency in a 1980 landslide over Jimmy Carter and a 1984 landslide over Walter Mondale. His presidency is defined by "Reaganomics" (the Kemp-Roth tax cut), breaking the PATCO air traffic controllers' strike, a hawkish anti-Soviet posture ("evil empire," the Strategic Defense Initiative, the Brandenburg Gate "tear down this wall" speech) that gave way to summitry with Mikhail Gorbachev, the invasion of Grenada, air strikes on Libya, and the Iran-Contra affair. Survived a 1981 assassination attempt by John Hinckley Jr. that left press secretary James Brady paralyzed and later prompted the Brady gun-control law.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Reagan is identified in giveaways: the fortieth U.S. president, a former Hollywood actor nicknamed "the Great Communicator" and "the Gipper," whose presidency ran through most of the 1980s and was ultimately "tarnished" by the Iran-Contra scandal.

clues.clue: Fortieth president of the United States, in office 1981-1989
examples: This U.S. president ordered the invasion of Grenada in Operation Urgent Fury after Bishop's overthrow in 1983. He quipped that he "forgot to duck" after being shot by a guy trying to impress Jodie Foster.
This governor of California once presided over the Screen Actors Guild. He was president from 1981-1989.

clues.clue: Nicknamed the "Great Communicator" for his speaking prowess, and "the Gipper" from an earlier acting role
examples: For 10 points, name this man, whose speaking prowess led him to be dubbed the "Great Communicator".
During its Invasion of Grenada, the United States was led by this mediocre actor-turned-president from 1981-1989...(accept "The Gipper")

clues.clue: A Republican whose presidency is frequently described as "tarnished" by the Iran-Contra scandal
examples: FTP, name this president who proposed the Strategic Defense Initiative, a Republican tarnished by the Iran-Contra scandal.
the Iran-Contra affair occurred during what Republican president's second term?

works.name: Hollywood Career & Screen Actors Guild
works.description: Before politics, Reagan was a film actor who rose to lead the Screen Actors Guild, a role that put him at the center of Hollywood's anti-communist blacklist era and gave him an early platform for conservative causes.

clues.clue: Served as president of the Screen Actors Guild
examples: This governor of California once presided over the Screen Actors Guild. He was president from 1981-1989.

clues.clue: In 1947, testified before HUAC about receiving Screen Actors Guild literature tied to a "Committee for a Far-Eastern Democratic Policy," and under the FBI codename "T-10" provided names of suspected communist sympathizers
examples: In 1947, this man testified that he received literature supporting a "Committee for a Far-Eastern Democratic Policy" from the Screen Actors Guild. Under the codename "T-10," this man provided names of potential communist sympathizers to the FBI.

clues.clue: As a celebrity spokesman, recorded an LP for the American Medical Association's anti-Medicare campaign "Operation Coffee Cup," titled Ronald Reagan Speaks Out Against Socialized Medicine
examples: During its anti-Medicare campaign Operation Coffee Cup, the American Medical Association held coffee meetings where they played an LP recorded by this celebrity in which he Speaks Out Against Socialized Medicine.

clues.clue: Political debut came with the "A Time for Choosing" speech in support of Barry Goldwater's 1964 campaign
examples: His political debut came with his "A Time for Choosing" speech in support of Barry Goldwater.

works.name: Governor of California (1967-1975)
works.description: Reagan's first elected office, won amid backlash to Pat Brown's perceived weak response to the Watts riots, saw him clash with Berkeley student protesters, sign notable pre-Roe abortion and mental-health legislation, and crack down on the armed Black Panther "copwatching" movement.

clues.clue: Won the governorship of California in 1966 after incumbent Pat Brown's response to the Watts riots was perceived as weak, and won a second term by defeating "Big Daddy" (Jesse) Unruh
examples: Brown's response to the Watts riots was perceived as weak, thus allowing this future president to win the governorship.
He won a second term as governor by defeating "Big Daddy" Unruh.

clues.clue: As governor, dealt with Berkeley student protests, put down the People's Park protest on "Bloody Thursday," and largely wiped out predecessor Pat Brown's budget deficit
examples: This governor dealt with Berkeley student protests and largely wiped out the budget deficit of his predecessor, Pat Brown.
As governor, he put down the People's Park Protest on Bloody Thursday.

clues.clue: Signed the Mulford Act (1967), a gun-control law that became national news after armed Black Panthers occupied the California State Capitol to protest it, cracking down on "copwatching"
examples: This politician cracked down on "copwatching" by signing into law the Mulford Act, which became national news when armed Black Panthers occupied the State Capitol.

clues.clue: In his first year as governor, signed the pre-Roe Therapeutic Abortion Act into law
examples: In his first year as governor, he signed the pre-Roe Therapeutic Abortion Act into law.

clues.clue: Signed the Lanterman-Petris-Short Act, which made it much harder to involuntarily commit the mentally ill, after complaining about politicians like Joseph S. Clark Jr. calling ordinary Americans "the masses"
examples: This politician signed the Lanterman-Petris-Short Act which made it much harder to commit mentally insane people.

works.name: Presidential Campaigns & Elections (1980, 1984)
works.description: Reagan won the presidency from Jimmy Carter in 1980 partly by mobilizing Kevin Phillips's "Southern strategy" and coded appeals to states' rights, then crushed Walter Mondale in a 1984 landslide behind the "Morning in America" ad campaign.

clues.clue: Declared his belief in "states' rights" at the Neshoba County Fair (near where three CORE civil rights workers had been murdered in 1964); the New York Times's Paul Krugman later attributed the phrase's use to "tacit race-baiting," and Reagan repeatedly invoked welfare-fraud figure Linda Taylor during the campaign
examples: This man told Neshoba County Fair that the "answer to any problem lies with the people" because he believed in "states rights."
During a political campaign, this man repeatedly alluded to a perpetrator of fraud named Linda Taylor.

clues.clue: Defeated Jimmy Carter in the 1980 election, aided by strategist Kevin Phillips's "Southern strategy" and endorsed by foreign-policy adviser Jeane Kirkpatrick, then famously retorted "There you go again" during a debate
examples: This president fired the striking air traffic controllers. He defeated Jimmy Carter in the 1980 election.
This man condescendingly repeated the line "There you go again" during a debate.

clues.clue: Won the first-ever NRA presidential endorsement
examples: This politician was the first presidential candidate to earn an NRA endorsement.

clues.clue: Defeated Walter Mondale and running mate Geraldine Ferraro in a 1984 landslide (49 states), running ads about a "bear in the woods" and declaring it "morning again in America"
examples: Mondale teamed up with Geraldine Ferraro on the Democratic ticket in the 1984 election, but lost 49 states to this incumbent president who invoked the image of "Morning in America."
This politician's campaign advertisements included one about a "bear in the woods" and another one proclaiming it was "morning again in America."

clues.clue: His first speech as president concluded with a story about Martin Treptow, who died fighting in World War I; that inaugural address, written by Ken Khachigian, declared "government is not the solution to our problems; government is the problem"
examples: This man's first speech as president concluded with a story about Martin Treptow, who died fighting in World War I.
In this man's first inaugural address, which was written by Ken Khachigian, he controversially stated that "government is not the solution to our problems; government is the problem."

works.name: 1981 Assassination Attempt
works.description: Two months into his first term, Reagan survived a shooting by John Hinckley Jr., who was obsessed with actress Jodie Foster; the attack left press secretary James Brady permanently paralyzed and later inspired federal gun-control legislation bearing Brady's name.

clues.clue: Was shot by John Hinckley Jr. outside the Washington Hilton Hotel (after Reagan had addressed the AFL-CIO), an attack Hinckley carried out to impress actress Jodie Foster, whom he had become obsessed with after watching Taxi Driver
examples: For 10 points, name this president who survived an assassination attempt months after his inauguration in 1981.
John Hinckley had been trying to impress this actress, who he became obsessed with after watching Taxi Driver multiple times.

clues.clue: Press secretary James Brady was shot and left permanently paralyzed in the attack, prompting Secretary of State Alexander Haig to declare "I am in control here" at a White House press conference
examples: This president's Secretary of State declared "I am in control here" after a failed assassination attempt on his life by John Hinckley.
For ten points, name this American President whose Secretary of State, Al Haig, once sparked rumors of a coup by saying "I am in control here".

clues.clue: Reportedly joked "Honey, I forgot to duck" to wife Nancy Reagan after being shot
examples: The joke "Honey, I forgot to duck" was said to this woman by her husband after he was shot and wounded.

clues.clue: The shooting of James Brady eventually led to the 1993 Brady Handgun Violence Prevention Act, which mandated federal background checks on firearm purchases
examples: This president's press secretary is the namesake of a 1993 bill that mandated federal background checks on firearm purchases; that bill is the Brady Law.
Name this former White House Press Secretary who names an act that requires firearms buyers to undergo federal background checks.

works.name: Reaganomics & Domestic Economic Policy
works.description: Reagan's signature economic program combined large tax cuts with deregulation, implemented with the help of OMB Director David Stockman, and coincided with the deployment of Peacekeeper missiles and the onset of the Savings and Loan crisis.

clues.clue: Signed the Kemp-Roth tax cut into law and enacted his deregulatory economic policy ("Reaganomics") with the help of OMB Director David Stockman
examples: He signed the Kemp-Roth tax cut into law, and enacted his economic policy w help of his Director of the OMB, David Stockman.

clues.clue: His administration saw the first deployment of Peacekeeper missiles, the Gulf of Sidra incident, and the beginning of the Savings and Loan crisis
examples: His administration saw the first deployment of Peacekeeper missiles, the Gulf of Sidra incident, the beginning of the Savings and Loan crisis.

clues.clue: After he left office, his HUD Secretary Samuel Pierce was implicated in a low-income housing scandal, and his Interior Secretary James Watt controversially banned a Grass Roots (and Beach Boys) concert on the National Mall
examples: After this president left office, his HUD Secretary, Samuel Pierce, was implicated in a low-income housing scandal.
His Interior Secretary controversially banned a Grass Roots concert on the Mall and was named James Watt.

clues.clue: Defused the Sagebrush Rebellion (a movement to free up federally-held Western land) by appointing James Watt as Interior Secretary, having declared himself a "sagebrush rebel" while campaigning in Salt Lake City
examples: While campaigning in Salt Lake City, this future president declared himself a "sagebrush rebel."

works.name: PATCO Air Traffic Controllers' Strike (1981)
works.description: Despite having received the union's endorsement in 1980, Reagan broke the Professional Air Traffic Controllers Organization strike by firing thousands of controllers en masse, a defining moment for American organized labor.

clues.clue: Broke the PATCO strike by firing most of the nation's air traffic controllers after a 48-hour warning, despite having received the union's endorsement in the 1980 election; the fired controllers (led by Robert E. Poli) received a lifetime civil-service ban, later lifted by Bill Clinton in 1993
examples: This President broke the PATCO strike by firing most of the nation's air traffic controllers, despite receiving PATCO's support in the 1980 presidential election the year before.
This man condescendingly repeated the line "There you go again" during a debate, and he broke a nationwide Air Traffic Controller's Strike.

works.name: Civil Rights & South Africa Policy
works.description: Reagan's civil-rights record drew sustained criticism: he escalated the War on Drugs against Black communities, appointed controversial civil-rights officials, nominated Robert Bork to the Supreme Court, and pursued a policy of "constructive engagement" with apartheid South Africa.

clues.clue: Escalated the War on Drugs against Black communities as a two-term president
examples: name this two-term president, who escalated the War on Drugs against Black communities.

clues.clue: His Commission on Civil Rights was chaired by Clarence Pendleton, who claimed comparable worth was "the looniest idea since Looney Tunes"
examples: Under this politician, the Commission on Civil Rights was chaired by Clarence Pendleton, who claimed that comparable worth was "the looniest idea since Looney Tunes."

clues.clue: Nominated Robert Bork to the Supreme Court, a pick Ted Kennedy attacked by claiming "blacks would sit at segregated lunch counters"
examples: Ted Kennedy claimed that "blacks would sit at segregated lunch counters" after this president nominated Robert Bork to the Supreme Court.

clues.clue: Pursued "constructive engagement" with apartheid South Africa (formulated by adviser Chester Crocker), controversially met with UNITA leader Jonas Savimbi, and vetoed the more punitive Dellums Amendment
examples: This politician received backlash for the lack of punitive measures in his policy of "constructive engagement" with South Africa.
On the advice of Chet Crocker, this president vetoed the Dellums Amendment, favoring his own more restrained policy of "constructive engagement."

clues.clue: Controversially placed a wreath at the Bitburg military cemetery, which included graves of SS members, and claimed the SS were "also victims" of the Nazis
examples: This leader controversially placed a wreath in a cemetery honoring SS members in Bitburg.
He claimed that SS members were also victims of the Nazis during a trip to the Bitburg Military Cemetery.

clues.clue: Joked about food aid to the poor being "botulism ridden," mocking the Symbionese Liberation Army's demand for food distribution after the kidnapping of Patty Hearst
examples: This politician joked about food for the poor being botulism ridden in order to mock domestic terrorists.
After the Symbionese Liberation Army demanded the distribution of food following the kidnapping of Patty Hearst, this man joked about a botulism outbreak contaminating the food.

works.name: Cold War Diplomacy with the Soviet Union
works.description: Reagan's Soviet policy pivoted from confrontational rhetoric and a major arms buildup in his first term to landmark summitry with Mikhail Gorbachev, culminating in the INF Treaty.

clues.clue: At the Brandenburg Gate in West Berlin, told Mikhail Gorbachev "If you seek liberalization... tear down this wall!"
examples: In a speech at Brandenburg Gate, one of these leaders told the other "If you seek liberalization... tear down this wall!"
This American president ordered the Libya air strikes. He told Mikhail Gorbachev to tear down a wall.

clues.clue: Called the Soviet Union an "evil empire" and predicted it would be relegated to the "ash heap of history"
examples: For 10 points, name this President who called the Soviet Union an "evil empire" and told Mikhail Gorbachev to tear down the Berlin Wall.
whose claims that the USSR would be relegated to the "ash heap of history" and promotion of SDI made Soviets wary during Able Archer.

clues.clue: Proposed the Strategic Defense Initiative ("Star Wars"), a controversial missile-defense program using lasers and particle beams, which he clashed over with Gorbachev at their negotiations
examples: The Strategic Defense Initiative was announced by this president.
These signers of the INF treaty clashed over the SDI program.

clues.clue: Blasted the Soviet Union after it shot down Korean Air Lines Flight 007 in 1983
examples: This leader blasted the Soviet Union after the shooting down of Korean Airlines Flight 007.
Chernenko held power during the administration of this American president, who used the term "evil empire" to refer to the Soviet Union

clues.clue: Held summits with Mikhail Gorbachev, including at Reykjavik, where negotiations stalled over the Brilliant Pebbles component of SDI; Suzanne Massie suggested he use the phrase "trust, but verify" with Gorbachev, and they later signed the INF Treaty
examples: Negotiations between these people stalled at the Reykjavik Summit, where one demanded that the other confine work on the Brilliant Pebbles system to the laboratory.
Suzanne Massie suggested that one of these people use the phrase "trust, but verify" with the other.

clues.clue: His hawkish rhetoric and SDI program contributed to Soviet paranoia during the 1983 NATO exercise Able Archer 83, which came close to being mistaken for a real first-strike preparation
examples: whose claims that the USSR would be relegated to the "ash heap of history" and promotion of SDI made Soviets wary during Able Archer.

works.name: Interventions Abroad: Grenada & Libya
works.description: Reagan ordered two major unilateral military actions: the 1983 invasion of Grenada following a Marxist coup, and 1986 air strikes on Libya in retaliation for a terrorist bombing in Berlin.

clues.clue: Ordered the invasion of Grenada (Operation Urgent Fury) in 1983, overthrowing coup leader Hudson Austin after the execution of Prime Minister Maurice Bishop; some claimed it was timed to distract from the concurrent Beirut barracks bombing
examples: This president authorized the overthrow of Hudson Austin in Operation Urgent Fury, the invasion of Grenada.
The invasion of Grenada was spurred by the execution of this Communist Prime Minister of Grenada.

clues.clue: Ordered the 1986 bombing of Libya (Operation El Dorado Canyon) in retaliation for the bombing of a Berlin discotheque
examples: This American president ordered the Libya air strikes. He told Mikhail Gorbachev to tear down a wall.
The bombing of Libya was ordered in retaliation for the bombing of a discotheque in this city.

works.name: Iran-Contra Affair & Central America Policy
works.description: Reagan's Central America policy, driven by fierce anti-communism, produced his administration's defining scandal: covertly selling arms to Iran and diverting the proceeds to fund Nicaraguan Contra rebels in violation of the Boland Amendment, while also backing repressive anti-communist regimes in Guatemala and El Salvador.

clues.clue: Staffer Oliver North helped run a scheme, alongside Robert McFarlane and (later) witness Fawn Hall, that sold arms to Iran and diverted proceeds to fund the Contras fighting Daniel Ortega's Sandinista government in Nicaragua, circumventing the Boland Amendment
examples: For 10 points, name this president and arms dealer whose staff member Oliver North was implicated in the Iran-Contra affair.
That plan, supported by this President, initially sold arms to Iranians, but then provided money to forces that fought against the Daniel Ortega-led Sandinista government in Nicaragua.

clues.clue: Iranian intermediaries Manouchehr Ghorbanifar and Mehdi Hashemi were involved in the scandal; Hashemi leaked news of the Boland Amendment breach, and Reagan later admitted "mistakes were made"
examples: Mehdi Hashemi leaked to the media that this leader breached the Boland Amendment, though this leader claimed that "mistakes were made" after his administration was implicated in trading "arms for hostages."

clues.clue: A plane flown by Eugene Hasenfus, ferrying supplies to the Contras, was shot down, dealing a major diplomatic blow; Defense Secretary Caspar Weinberger was later indicted (and resigned) over the scandal, which broke during Reagan's second term
examples: A major diplomatic blow for this man occurred when Eugene Hasenfus' plane was shot down.
This man's Secretary of Defense, Caspar Weinberger, was indicted following the Iran-Contra scandal that occurred during his second term as President.

clues.clue: In Guatemala, endorsed dictator Efrain Rios Montt as a "man of great integrity" with a "bum rap" (a day before the Dos Erres massacre) and circumvented a military-aid ban on the earlier Lucas Garcia regime, whose aircraft were used in the "Silent Holocaust"
examples: A day after this leader endorsed Efrain Rios Montt as a "man of great integrity" who had a "bum rap," a massacre occurred in the Ixil village of Dos Erres.
This leader circumvented a ban on military aid to the Lucas Garcia regime, which included aircraft used in the "Silent Holocaust."

clues.clue: In El Salvador, gave military support to a regime responsible for the El Mozote massacre
examples: This man gave military support to a regime responsible for the El Mozote Massacre.

clues.clue: His reputation was boosted early on when the Iran Hostage Crisis ended on his Inauguration Day
examples: This president's reputation was boosted after he ended the Iran Hostage Crisis.

works.name: Gun Legislation
works.description: Reagan's relationship with gun policy spanned his governorship to his presidency: he signed a gun-control law targeting the Black Panthers as governor, then as president signed a federal law that (almost inadvertently) banned new civilian machine gun sales.

clues.clue: Signed the Firearm Owners' Protection Act (1986) into law, which paradoxically included Representative William Hughes's amendment banning civilian sales of newly manufactured machine guns; the bill narrowly passed after Reagan's handwritten note flipped Representative Scott Klug's vote, and it included a 10-year sunset provision
examples: This politician's handwritten note to Representative Scott Klug flipped Klug's vote and secured a narrow passage of a bill that was later terminated by a 10-year sunset provision.
This politician signed into law a bill that paradoxically included William Hughes's amendment to prevent civilian sales of machine guns.

works.name: Legacy & Cultural Depictions
works.description: Reagan's afterlife includes an airport renamed in his honor, a starring (unflattering) role in works by playwright Tony Kushner and novelist J. G. Ballard, and enduring public fascination with First Lady Nancy Reagan's use of a personal astrologer.

clues.clue: Washington National Airport was renamed Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport in his honor
examples: name this airport which lies across the Potomac River from the nation's capital.

clues.clue: Tony Kushner's play Angels in America is set during his presidential administration, portrayed there as having restored the country's "sacred position among nations"
examples: For 10 points, Angels in America is set during what presidential administration?

clues.clue: Is the (unnamed but obvious) subject of J. G. Ballard's short story "Why I Want to Fuck Ronald Reagan," from The Atrocity Exhibition, a mock scientific paper on his sexual appeal
examples: name this short story from The Atrocity Exhibition by J. G. Ballard, written as a scientific paper about the sexual appeal of a former Republican president.

clues.clue: Wife Nancy Reagan infamously consulted a personal astrologer named Joan Quigley and coined the anti-drug slogan "Just Say No"; Chief of Staff Donald Regan revealed her astrologer use in his 1988 memoir after clashing with her
examples: Name this woman who infamously consulted a personal astrologer named Joan Quigley.
This Chief of Staff for Reagan reportedly quarreled with the First Lady and embarrassingly revealed her use of an astrologer in his 1988 memoir.

comprehensive_summary: Ronald Reagan (1911-2004) was a Hollywood actor and Screen Actors Guild president (who testified before HUAC in 1947 and secretly informed for the FBI under the codename "T-10") before entering politics with his 1964 "A Time for Choosing" speech for Barry Goldwater. As Governor of California (1967-1975), elected after Pat Brown's perceived weak response to the Watts riots and re-elected over "Big Daddy" Unruh, he clashed with Berkeley protesters (suppressing the People's Park protest on "Bloody Thursday"), signed the pre-Roe Therapeutic Abortion Act and the Lanterman-Petris-Short Act, and signed the Mulford Act after armed Black Panthers occupied the State Capitol. He won the presidency from Jimmy Carter in 1980, aided by Kevin Phillips's Southern strategy and coded "states' rights" appeals at the Neshoba County Fair, then crushed Walter Mondale and Geraldine Ferraro in a 49-state 1984 landslide behind "Morning in America" advertising. Two months into his first term he survived an assassination attempt by John Hinckley Jr. (obsessed with actress Jodie Foster), which left press secretary James Brady paralyzed, prompted Secretary of State Alexander Haig's "I am in control here" remark, and eventually led to the 1993 Brady gun-control law; as president he had also signed the Firearm Owners' Protection Act, whose Hughes Amendment banned new civilian machine gun sales. His economic program, "Reaganomics," centered on the Kemp-Roth tax cut and deregulation under OMB Director David Stockman. In 1981 he broke the PATCO air traffic controllers' strike by firing thousands of controllers en masse. His civil-rights record drew sustained criticism for escalating the War on Drugs against Black communities, nominating Robert Bork to the Supreme Court, and pursuing "constructive engagement" with apartheid South Africa; he also controversially laid a wreath at the Bitburg cemetery, which included SS graves. His anti-Soviet rhetoric — calling the USSR an "evil empire" bound for the "ash heap of history," proposing the Strategic Defense Initiative ("Star Wars"), and condemning the 1983 shootdown of Korean Air Lines Flight 007 — gave way to landmark summitry with Mikhail Gorbachev, including the tense Reykjavik Summit and his Brandenburg Gate demand to "tear down this wall," culminating in the INF Treaty. He ordered the 1983 invasion of Grenada (Operation Urgent Fury) after the execution of Maurice Bishop and the 1986 bombing of Libya (Operation El Dorado Canyon) in retaliation for a Berlin discotheque bombing. His presidency's defining scandal, the Iran-Contra affair, saw aide Oliver North and others sell arms to Iran and divert the proceeds to fund Nicaraguan Contra rebels in violation of the Boland Amendment, a scheme that unraveled after Eugene Hasenfus's supply plane was shot down and led to Defense Secretary Caspar Weinberger's indictment; his Central America policy also included military support for regimes implicated in the El Mozote and Dos Erres massacres. Washington National Airport was later renamed in his honor, and his administration became a fixture of American drama and fiction, from Tony Kushner's Angels in America to J. G. Ballard's "Why I Want to Fuck Ronald Reagan." First Lady Nancy Reagan became known for the "Just Say No" anti-drug campaign and her consultation of astrologer Joan Quigley.
recursive_suggestions: Ronald Reagan and Mikhail Gorbachev — recurring compound answerline (Reykjavik, INF Treaty, 'trust but verify') distinct from Reagan alone; watch for double-counting in cross-refs
Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport — recurring standalone Other Academic answerline about the airport itself, not the man; excluded from biographical clues here
Iran-Contra affair — could merit its own Events topic distinct from Reagan the figure, given the density of named participants (Oliver North, Fawn Hall, Robert McFarlane, John Poindexter)
Invasion of Grenada / Operation Urgent Fury — recurring standalone answerline with independent bonus-part presence
PATCO strike — recurring standalone bonus-part answerline ('air traffic controllers', 2x)
Jeane Kirkpatrick — Reagan's UN Ambassador and foreign-policy adviser, recurring figure with her own answerline potential
Nancy Reagan — has independent bonus-part presence (astrologer, 'Just Say No'); could merit a standalone topic
Angels in America and 'Why I Want to Fuck Ronald Reagan' — Literature answerlines that use Reagan as subject matter; excluded here as separate topics
U.S. Presidential debates — unrelated general-answerline tossup that surfaced in the raw search (Reagan-Carter debate is only one example among several); excluded entirely
